perturbation.original_text: Seller hereby covenants and agrees, that from time to time forthwith upon the reasonable written request of Backhaul or Buyer, that Seller will, at Backhaul's cost and expense, do, execute, acknowledge and deliver or cause to be done, executed, acknowledged and delivered, each and all of such further acts, deeds, assignments, transfers, conveyances and assurances as may reasonably be required by Backhaul or Buyer in order to transfer, assign, convey and deliver unto and vest in Backhaul title to all right, title and interest of Seller in, to and under the Assigned Intellectual Property.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller hereby covenants and agrees, that from time to time upon the request of Backhaul or Buyer, that Seller will, at Backhaul's cost and expense, do, execute, acknowledge and deliver or cause to be done, executed, acknowledged and delivered, each and all of such further acts, deeds, assignments, transfers, conveyances and assurances as may reasonably be required by Backhaul or Buyer in order to transfer, assign, convey and deliver unto and vest in Backhaul title to all right, title and interest of Seller in, to and under the Assigned Intellectual Property. Any such request must be fulfilled within 1 business day.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces a requirement that Seller fulfill requests related to intellectual property transfer within 1 business day, which is an unreasonably short timeframe and potentially conflicts with the 'reasonable' standard often applied to contract performance under Georgia law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Georgia law requires contracts to be performed in good faith and with reasonable diligence. Setting an inflexible deadline of 1 business day for complex tasks such as executing legal documents may be deemed unreasonable, especially considering the potential need for legal review and internal approvals. This modification removes the term 'reasonable' from the time requirement, and introduces an unrealistically short, strict deadline, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract performance. O.C.G.A. § 13-4-20 requires parties to perform their contractual obligations in good faith.
perturbation.location: ASSIGNMENT section, paragraph starting with 'Seller hereby covenants'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 13-4-102. Stat. 2022 Colorado Rev. Stat. 2016 Colorado Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2016 Colorado Revised Statutes Title 13 - Courts and Court Procedure Courts of Record Article 4 - Court of Appeals § 13-4-102. Jurisdiction CO Rev Stat § 13-4-102 (2016) What's This? (1) Any provision of law to the contrary notwithstanding, the court of appeals shall have initial jurisdiction over appeals from final judgments of, and interlocutory appeals of certified questions of law in civil cases pursuant to section 13-4-102.1 from, the district courts, the probate court of the city and county of Denver, and the juvenile court of the city and county of Denver, except in: (a) Repealed. (b) Cases in which a statute, a municipal charter provision, or an ordinance has been declared unconstitutional; (c) Cases concerned with decisions or actions of the public utilities commission; (d) Water cases involving priorities or adjudications; (e) Writs of habeas corpus; (f) Cases appealed from the county court to the district court, as provided in section 13-6-310; (g) Summary proceedings initiated under articles 1 to 13.5 of title 1 and article 10 of title 31, C.R.S.; (h) Cases appealed from the district court granting or denying postconviction relief in a case in which a sentence of death has been imposed. (2) The court of appeals has initial jurisdiction to: (a) Review awards or actions of the industrial claim appeals office, as provided in articles 43 and 74 of title 8, C.R.S.; (b) Review orders of the banking board granting or denying charters for new state banks, as provided in article 102 of title 11, C.R.S.; (c) (Deleted by amendment, L. 2006, p. 761, § 19, effective July 1, 2006.) (d) Review all final actions and orders appropriate for judicial review of the Co
perturbation.justification: The change from 'forthwith upon the reasonable written request' to 'upon the request' and the addition of 'must be fulfilled within 1 business day' introduces a strict, potentially unreasonable, deadline. This contradicts the general principle of good faith and reasonable diligence in contract performance, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 pertains to Colorado jurisdiction and is not directly relevant, the Law Explanation correctly points out that Georgia law (O.C.G.A. § 13-4-20, though not provided) requires good faith, and the one-day deadline undermines that principle by potentially making compliance impossible or requiring unreasonable effort. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition accurately describes this scenario, where a time-sensitive obligation is set that potentially violates state law regarding reasonable performance.

perturbation.original_text: This Intellectual Property Agreement shall be construed and interpreted according to the laws of the State of Georgia, applicable contracts to be wholly performed within the State of Georgia.
perturbation.changed_text: This Intellectual Property Agreement shall be construed and interpreted according to the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Georgia to Delaware could be problematic if the contract is to be performed wholly within Georgia, particularly if Georgia law provides specific protections or regulations related to intellectual property that Delaware law does not. By specifying Delaware law 'without regard to its conflict of laws principles,' this attempts to force Delaware law to apply even if Georgia has the most significant relationship to the transaction. Note that the parties are incorporated in Delaware and Georgia.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia Choice of Law Rules; Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187
perturbation.law_explanation: Georgia courts generally respect the parties' choice of governing law unless the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no other reasonable basis for the parties' choice, or application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. Since the contract mentions that it applies to contracts 'wholly performed within the State of Georgia,' Georgia might have stronger ties to the contract. By explicitly stating 'without regard to its conflict of laws principles' the contract attempts to force Delaware law on the transaction, which might go against the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 as adopted and interpreted by Georgia courts.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'This Intellectual Property Agreement shall be construed'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The altered contract language designates Delaware law to govern the agreement, irrespective of conflict of law principles, while the original agreement stated Georgia law would govern. The perturbation explanation and law explanation suggest this is problematic because the contract pertains to actions 'wholly performed within the State of Georgia,' potentially giving Georgia a stronger claim under conflict of laws principles. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' By explicitly disregarding conflict of laws, the contract attempts to circumvent the typical legal process for determining the appropriate governing law, creating an inconsistency that could render the choice of Delaware law unenforceable, especially since the parties are incorporated in Delaware and Georgia.

perturbation.original_text: Delivery of an executed counterpart of a signature page to this Intellectual Property Agreement by facsimile shall be effective as delivery of a manually executed counterpart of this Intellectual Property Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Delivery of an executed counterpart of a signature page to this Intellectual Property Agreement by facsimile shall NOT be effective as delivery of a manually executed counterpart of this Intellectual Property Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the acceptance of facsimile signatures to non-acceptance may be problematic. Although Georgia law does allow for electronic signatures, excluding fax signatures as valid could impede the contract's enforceability, especially if it contradicts standard business practices and Georgia's Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA), which generally recognizes electronic signatures.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 10-12-4 states that an electronic record or electronic signature is attributable to a person if it was the act of the person. Although it does not specifically include facsimile as an electronic signature, by disallowing it, the clause may impede the intended meaning of the Georgia UETA. The change to not allow for facsimile could be interpreted as a lack of good faith. Under Georgia law, if the signing parties are in different locations, a signature page delivered via fax transmission is considered a valid, legally binding instrument.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'Delivery of an executed counterpart'

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that facsimile signatures are NOT effective, directly contradicting the original text which states they ARE effective. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is just a login page to LexisNexis and doesn't provide specific legal content, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that this change may impede the contract's enforceability, especially considering Georgia's UETA and standard business practices. Although no specific law snippet supports the change, the change removes the acceptance of a previously accepted signature method, and this inconsistency is problematic because the original text had a valid acceptance of the facsimile signature.
